Recorder Documentation

Overview

The Recorder container is a digital waveguide physical model of a recorder (fipple flute), based on the algorithm developed by Perry R. Cook and Gary P. Scavone in the Synthesis ToolKit (STK). It models the acoustic behavior of a cylindrical air column driven by a jet of air through a fipple: breath pressure excites a jet nonlinearity, which drives a bore resonance via two coupled delay lines (bore and jet), producing the characteristic woody, breathy tone of a soprano or alto recorder.

Because the model is self-oscillating, it produces a continuous pitched tone for the full duration of any note. The pitch is tracked continuously, so pitch curves and portamento in the Score Canvas work without artifacts. Legato transitions preserve the bore's vibrating state, allowing smooth slurs between notes.

Category

Essential (Blue) - Core sound generation

Ports

Input Ports

Port Data Type Default Description
breathPressure Control 0.81 Breath pressure driving the jet (0.0–1.0)
jetRatio Control 0.35 Jet delay length as a fraction of the bore delay (0.05–0.60)
noiseGain Control 0.015 Breath turbulence noise amplitude (0.0–0.5)
vibratoFreq Control 0.0 Vibrato oscillator frequency in Hz (0.0–20.0)
vibratoGain Control 0.0 Vibrato depth — 0.0 disables vibrato entirely (0.0–1.0)
endReflection Control 0.73 Bore open-end reflection coefficient (−1.0–1.0)
jetReflection Control 0.59 Jet feedback reflection coefficient (−1.0–1.0)
pitchMultiplier Control 1.0 Scales the note frequency before synthesis. 1.0 = no change, 2.0 = octave up, 0.5 = octave down. Any ratio works — the system is tuning-agnostic. Can be driven by LFO (vibrato), Envelope Engine (pitch glide), or Physics System (spring pitch wobble).

Output Ports

Port Data Type Description
signalOut Signal Generated recorder tone

Parameters

Parameter Type Default Range Description
breathPressure Float 0.81 0.0–1.0 Overall blowing strength
jetRatio Float 0.35 0.05–0.60 Jet delay relative to bore delay
noiseGain Float 0.015 0.0–0.5 Breath noise / breathiness
vibratoFreq Float 0.0 0.0–20.0 Vibrato rate in Hz
vibratoGain Float 0.0 0.0–1.0 Vibrato depth (0.0 = off)
endReflection Float 0.73 −1.0–1.0 Bore open-end termination
jetReflection Float 0.59 −1.0–1.0 Jet-to-bore feedback amount
pitchMultiplier Float 1.0 0.25–4.0 Pitch ratio applied before synthesis (1.0 = no change, 2.0 = octave up, 0.5 = octave down)

How It Works

The algorithm is a digital waveguide simulation of the flute/recorder acoustics. Each sample is computed as follows:

breathP      = breathPressure × envelope + breathP × noiseGain × noise()
             + vibratoGain × 0.05 × sin(vibratoPhase)

temp         = −onePole( boreDelay.lastOut )

pressureDiff = breathP − jetReflection × temp
pressureDiff = jetDelay.tick( pressureDiff )
pressureDiff = dcBlock( jetTable( pressureDiff ) ) × 1.2
             + endReflection × temp × 1.02

output       = 0.2 × boreDelay.tick( pressureDiff )

The key components are:

  • Bore delay: A linearly-interpolating delay line representing the cylindrical air column. Its length is tuned to the note's pitch (compensated for filter phase delay), making it the primary pitch-setting element.
  • Jet delay: A shorter delay line (length = jetRatio × borDelay) modeling the travel time of the air jet from the fipple to the edge. Its length sets the tone character and register.
  • Jet nonlinearity: The polynomial f(x) = x³ − x (clipped to ±1), modeling the aerodynamic instability of the jet at the labium edge. This is the energy source of the self-oscillation.
  • One-pole lowpass: Models the high-frequency losses at the bore's open end. Pole position is derived from the STK formula 0.7 − 0.1 × 2050 / sampleRate.
  • DC blocker: A high-pass filter (H(z) = (1 − z⁻¹) / (1 − 0.99z⁻¹)) applied after the jet table to remove any DC offset introduced by the nonlinearity.

Parameter Guide

Breath Pressure

Controls the overall driving force of the simulated breath. This is the most fundamental parameter — it determines whether the recorder oscillates at all, and at what amplitude.

  • Below ~0.65: Insufficient to sustain oscillation — the model underblows and produces little or no tone
  • 0.75–0.85: Characteristic recorder tone, warm and clear
  • Above ~0.92: Risk of register jump / overblowing — the model may jump to a higher harmonic mode

The parameter is extremely sensitive in the 0.75–0.90 range. Small increments (0.01–0.02) produce noticeable tonal changes.

Jet Ratio

Sets the jet delay length as a fraction of the bore delay. This is the second most sensitive parameter and has the greatest effect on tonal character and stability.

  • 0.20–0.28: Thinner, flute-like tone; slightly more breathy
  • 0.30–0.40: Full recorder character; woody and centred (default 0.35)
  • 0.45–0.55: Broader, rounder tone; more prominent fundamental
  • Above 0.55: Tonal instability — the oscillation becomes unstable at many pitches

Noise Gain

Adds breath turbulence noise multiplicatively to the breath pressure, simulating the aerodynamic noise present in real wind instrument playing.

  • 0.0: Pure, clean tone (unrealistically clean)
  • 0.010–0.025: Subtle breathiness — natural recorder character (default 0.015)
  • 0.05–0.15: Noticeably breathy, airy sound
  • 0.3–0.5: Strongly breathy — closer to a wind or breath effect than a pitched recorder

End Reflection

The reflection coefficient at the open end of the bore. Models how much of the pressure wave is reflected back into the tube when it reaches the open end. In a real recorder this is primarily set by the tube geometry.

  • Positive values (0.5–0.8): Standard open-tube behaviour; warm, full tone (default 0.73)
  • Lower values (0.3–0.5): More energy escapes — less resonance, slightly softer tone
  • Negative values: Inverted reflection (as in a closed tube); produces unstable or fundamentally altered oscillation

Jet Reflection

Controls how strongly the bore pressure feeds back into the jet delay, modelling the acoustic coupling between the bore and the jet near the fipple. Together with endReflection, it determines the overall loop gain and tonal brightness.

  • 0.5–0.65: Balanced, natural coupling (default 0.59)
  • 0.7–0.85: Higher feedback — can produce a more focused, projecting tone but risks instability at extreme pitches
  • Below 0.4: Weak coupling — tone may not sustain at lower breath pressures

Vibrato

Vibrato is disabled by default (vibratoGain = 0.0). When enabled, a sine wave modulates the breath pressure at vibratoFreq Hz with a depth proportional to vibratoGain.

  • Musical vibrato: vibratoFreq = 4.0–7.0 Hz, vibratoGain = 0.05–0.20
  • Fast tremolo: vibratoFreq = 8.0–15.0 Hz
  • vibratoFreq has no effect when vibratoGain = 0.0

Sensitivity Note

The Recorder parameters interact through a nonlinear feedback loop. Small changes in any parameter can have disproportionate effects on tone, register, and stability. The default values (0.81 / 0.35 / 0.015 / 0.73 / 0.59) represent a carefully balanced operating point for a clear, woody recorder tone. When experimenting:

  • Change one parameter at a time
  • Use step sizes of 0.01–0.02 for breath pressure, end/jet reflection
  • Use step sizes of 0.01–0.05 for jet ratio
  • Return to defaults if the tone becomes unstable or silent

Breath Envelope

The recorder applies a simple attack/release envelope to the breath pressure based on note progress:

  • Attack: 8% of note duration — ramps from 0 to full breath pressure. Simulates the onset of blowing. Skipped for legato notes.
  • Sustain: Full breath pressure.
  • Release: Last 5% of note duration — ramps back to zero. Simulates stopping the breath.

This envelope affects only the internal excitation; the overall dynamics of the note are controlled by the Score Canvas as usual.

Legato Behavior

For legato note transitions the bore and jet delay buffers are preserved. The oscillation continues uninterrupted from the previous note, with only the pitch (delay lengths) updated. The breath attack ramp is skipped so there is no re-onset transient between slurred notes.

For non-legato notes, both delay lines are fully cleared and the oscillation builds from silence at the start of each note.

Tail Behavior

Unlike string or membrane models, a wind instrument stops sounding almost immediately when blowing ceases. In tail mode (after a note ends), the jet algorithm is bypassed entirely and the bore delay buffer is simply drained to silence. This takes at most one bore-delay period (a few milliseconds), after which the audio engine detects silence and closes the tail cleanly.

Usage Patterns

Basic Recorder Tone

[Recorder] → signalOut → [Output]
  breathPressure: 0.81
  jetRatio:       0.35
  noiseGain:      0.015
  endReflection:  0.73
  jetReflection:  0.59

Breathy / Folk Recorder

breathPressure: 0.78
jetRatio:       0.33
noiseGain:      0.04
endReflection:  0.70
jetReflection:  0.56

Vibrato Expression

vibratoFreq:  5.5
vibratoGain:  0.12

Or modulate vibratoGain via an Envelope Engine to bring vibrato in gradually over the note duration.

Combining with Formant Body

[Recorder] → signalOut → [Formant Body] → signalOut → [Output]

The Formant Body processor can add vowel-like resonances to the recorder tone, useful for simulating the head joint or body resonances of specific instruments.

Combining with Reverb

[Recorder] → signalOut → [IR Convolution] → signalOut → [Output]
  wetDry: 0.3–0.5

A small room or hall IR adds natural acoustic space to the dry recorder tone.

External Vibrato via Pitch Multiplier

[LFO] → valueOut → pitchMultiplier → [Recorder]
  LFO frequency:  5.5 Hz
  LFO amplitude:  0.012
  LFO center:     1.0

Complements or replaces the internal vibrato (which modulates breath pressure). An external LFO on pitchMultiplier is a pure frequency vibrato, while the internal vibrato blends timbral and pitch movement together.

Troubleshooting

No Sound or Very Faint

  1. Increase breathPressure — must be above ~0.65 to sustain oscillation
  2. Check jetRatio is not below 0.10 or above 0.55
  3. Ensure endReflection and jetReflection are both positive and above 0.4

Overblowing / Wrong Register

  1. Reduce breathPressure — values above ~0.90 can cause a register jump
  2. Slightly reduce jetRatio (try 0.30–0.33)

Tone Too Thin or Buzzy

  1. Increase jetRatio toward 0.38–0.45
  2. Slightly reduce jetReflection (try 0.52–0.56)

Tone Too Dark or Hollow

  1. Reduce jetRatio toward 0.28–0.32
  2. Increase endReflection slightly (try 0.76–0.80)

Oscillation Unstable at Extreme Pitches

  1. Slightly reduce breathPressure and jetReflection
  2. The model is most stable in the middle register (roughly D4–D6 on a soprano recorder). Very low notes (<C4) and very high notes (>G6) are more sensitive to parameter values.

Technical Notes

Pitch Tracking

The bore delay length is recomputed whenever the pitch changes by more than 0.03%:

playFreq  = pitch × 0.96666        (slight overblowing correction)
boreSamps = sampleRate / playFreq − filterPhaseDelay(playFreq) − 1.0

The one-pole filter phase delay is subtracted to keep the oscillation accurately in tune. The jet delay length is updated simultaneously: jetDelay = boreSamps × jetRatio.

No STK Dependency

The model is implemented entirely within Kala (recordermodel.h / recordermodel.cpp) with no dependency on the STK library. All DSP components — the delay lines, one-pole filter, DC blocker, jet table, and LCG noise source — are self-contained.

Documentation accurate as of Kala v1.4. Source files: recordermodel.h, recordermodel.cpp. Algorithm based on STK Flute by Perry R. Cook & Gary P. Scavone, 1995–2021.
